attributes.stem: A 32-year-old woman at 34 weeks gestation presents to the Emergency Department following a motor vehicle collision. She has no significant medical history and takes only pregnancy vitamins. On examination, she appears distressed. Her pulse rate is 110/min, blood pressure 100/60 mmHg, respiratory rate 24/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.8°C. Her Glasgow Coma Scale score is 13/15. She requires urgent intubation for deteriorating conscious state.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following best describes the physiological changes the team need to be mindful of in this patient?
options: Increased functional residual capacity and reduced oxygen consumption is expected in pregnancy
Reduced airway oedema and increased gastric emptying is expected in pregnancy
Reduced oxygen consumption and normal gastric emptying is expected in pregnancy
Increased functional residual capacity and normal lower oesophageal tone is expected in pregnancy
Reduced functional residual capacity and delayed gastric emptying is expected in pregnancy
attributes.explanation:
This question addresses the important physiological changes in pregnancy that affect airway management. Several factors combine to make airway management more challenging and higher risk in pregnant patients.

Reduced functional residual capacity and delayed gastric emptying are key physiological changes that occur in pregnancy. The enlarging uterus pushes up the diaphragm, reducing functional residual capacity by up to 20%. Combined with a 20% increase in oxygen consumption, this leads to rapid desaturation during periods of apnoea. Progesterone also reduces lower oesophageal sphincter tone and delays gastric emptying, increasing aspiration risk.

Increased functional residual capacity and reduced oxygen consumption is incorrect - both changes occur in the opposite direction in pregnancy. The reduced functional residual capacity and increased oxygen consumption make pregnant patients particularly vulnerable to hypoxia.

Reduced airway oedema and increased gastric emptying is incorrect. Pregnancy causes increased airway oedema due to fluid retention and capillary engorgement, making the airway more difficult to visualise. Gastric emptying is delayed, not increased.

Reduced oxygen consumption and normal gastric emptying is incorrect. Oxygen consumption increases by about 20% in pregnancy, and gastric emptying is delayed due to progesterone effects.

Increased functional residual capacity and normal lower oesophageal tone is incorrect on both counts. Functional residual capacity is reduced, and lower oesophageal tone is decreased due to progesterone effects.

These physiological changes necessitate specific precautions during airway management, including pre-oxygenation, rapid sequence induction, cricoid pressure, and having a lower threshold for difficult airway management strategies.
learningPoints: Pregnant patients have reduced functional residual capacity and increased oxygen consumption, leading to rapid desaturation.
Pregnancy increases the risk of difficult airway due to airway oedema and breast enlargement.
Pregnant patients have delayed gastric emptying and reduced lower oesophageal sphincter tone, increasing aspiration risk.
references: Royal College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists (RCOG). Maternal collapse in pregnancy and the puerperium: Green-top guideline no. 56 [Internet]. London: RCOG; 2022 [cited 2024 Nov 22]. Available from: https://www.rcog.org.uk/guidance/browse-all-guidance/green-top-guidelines/maternal-collapse-in-pregnancy-and-the-puerperium-green-top-guideline-no-56/
